buggy_code:
import pandas as pd

# Two time periods with DatetimeIndex
df_2019 = pd.read_csv('2019.csv', index_col='date', parse_dates=True)
df_2020 = pd.read_csv('2020.csv', index_col='date', parse_dates=True)

# Concatenate preserving the DatetimeIndex
combined = pd.concat([df_2019, df_2020])

# Verify index is still a DatetimeIndex
assert isinstance(combined.index, pd.DatetimeIndex), 'Index lost after concat'

# Verify no duplicate dates
assert not combined.index.duplicated().any(), 'Duplicate dates found'

# Sort just in case
combined = combined.sort_index()

returns = combined['close'].pct_change().dropna()
clean_code:
import pandas as pd

df_2019 = pd.read_csv('2019.csv', index_col='date', parse_dates=True)
df_2020 = pd.read_csv('2020.csv', index_col='date', parse_dates=True)

combined = pd.concat([df_2019, df_2020])

assert isinstance(combined.index, pd.DatetimeIndex), 'Index lost after concat'
assert not combined.index.duplicated().any(), 'Duplicate dates found'

combined = combined.sort_index()

returns = combined['close'].pct_change().dropna()
description: Clean example — pd.concat without ignore_index, preserving DatetimeIndex with validation.
explanation: No ignore_index=True so DatetimeIndex is preserved. Assert checks confirm index type and no duplicates. sort_index() ensures chronological order. pct_change() correctly computes returns across the boundary.
